bullet_points: Load all .npy datasets and metadata.json from the provided absolute paths.
Standardize all trajectories to x(0)=0. For Pomeau-Manneville (PM) maps, discard the initial 500 steps to ensure steady-state intermittency.
Implement Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) to extract robust Hurst exponents H for each mechanism.
Fit the tail exponent alpha from the complementary CDF of step increments for each dataset.
Verify that the anomalous scaling regime spans at least 20% of the total trajectory length. Flag datasets that do not meet this criterion.
Explicitly print all DFA-derived Hurst exponents numerically to the console (with dataset name, H value, and scaling regime bounds), as well as the fitted alpha values.
Save the preprocessed data, DFA results, and fitted alpha values, and generate a plot of the DFA fluctuation functions.
sub_tasks.sub_task: Data Preprocessing, Tail Fitting, and Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA)

bullet_points: Calculate the local scaling exponent H(tau) = d(log sqrt(<x^2(tau)>)) / d(log tau) using a sliding window for ALL mechanism families (PM maps, CTRW, Lévy-Lorentz, Sisyphus, and pure Lévy stable).
Identify the crossover time tau_c where the system transitions from ballistic (H approx 1) to anomalous diffusion (H approx 1/alpha).
Use the persistence of the ballistic front at x approx +/- vt as a diagnostic to distinguish between Lévy walks and Lévy flights.
Save the crossover times and local scaling exponents. Generate plots of H(tau) vs tau for all datasets.
Compute the empirical probability density P(x, t) at various time steps.
For Sisyphus cooling datasets, calculate the Kurtosis and the Tsallis q-index of the central peak to quantify deviations from the Fractional Fokker-Planck (FFP) propagator.

Normalize the empirical probability densities P(x,t) by their respective time-dependent scaling factors t^(1/alpha) using a consistent alpha value (derived from the tail fitting in Step 1 or from known ground-truth parameters).
Compute the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between these normalized distributions and the pure Lévy-stable reference.
Print the KL divergence values numerically for each mechanism and time step to the console.
Save the computed metrics (Kurtosis, q-index, KL divergence) and generate plots of the normalized distributions.
For the Lévy-Lorentz gas datasets, explicitly detect the ballistic peaks in the propagator P(x, t) at x approx +/- vt.
Also compute the ballistic peak detection for the pure Lévy stable reference at matching alpha values for direct comparison.
Quantify the amplitude of these peaks relative to the central Lévy-stable core.
Use this ratio as an objective metric to classify the mechanism as Generalized Master Equation (GME)-governed (Lévy walk) or FFP-governed (Lévy flight).
Save the computed ratios and generate plots of P(x,t) highlighting the ballistic peaks and the central core.
sub_tasks.sub_task: Lévy Walk vs. Lévy Flight Classification

bullet_points: Estimate the local drift A(p) and diffusion B(p) using the parameters from metadata.json (gamma_0, p_0). Note that momentum p can be estimated from the position trajectories as dx/dt, requiring careful finite-difference handling given the discrete time steps.
Project the resulting effective noise back into position space to determine if the effective fractional operator is spatially dependent.
Correlate the derived fractional order alpha with the input physical parameters to link microscopic friction to the macroscopic operator.
Print the estimated effective alpha values for each Sisyphus cooling regime (strong, moderate, weak) alongside the theoretical prediction from the Tsallis q-index relationship alpha = (3-q)/(q-1).
Save the derived parameters and generate plots of the effective drift and diffusion.
Compute the empirical characteristic function phi(k, t) for the different mechanisms.
Calculate the ratio of phi(k, t) to the theoretical FFP characteristic function exp(-D_alpha |k|^alpha t).
Focus the analysis on the high-frequency regime where k > 1/L_corr (with L_corr as the correlation length) to identify the specific scales where the FFP approximation fails.

Estimate the generalized diffusion coefficient D_alpha empirically from the characteristic function fits (by fitting log|phi(k,t)| vs |k|^alpha t), and print the fitted D_alpha values for each mechanism.
Save the Fourier analysis results, fitted D_alpha values, and generate plots of the characteristic function ratios.
Aggregate all computed metrics: DFA-derived H, tail index alpha, KL-divergence, ballistic peak ratios, and Tsallis q-index.

Construct a classification matrix assigning each mechanism to its effective theory: Velocity-constrained (Lévy-Lorentz) -> GME, Friction-dominated (Sisyphus) -> Fractional Langevin, Jump-dominated (CTRW) -> FFP.

Compute and print a quantitative Lévy fidelity score for each mechanism — defined as 1 minus the mean KL divergence from the pure Lévy stable reference across all time steps — and rank the mechanisms by this score.
Generate summary plots mapping the KL-divergence as a function of the tail index alpha and the DFA-derived scaling exponent H to delineate universality classes.
Save the final aggregated dataset and the summary plots.
Write the full Results section of the paper or report.
Interpret the DFA and crossover analysis results, discussing the transition from ballistic to anomalous diffusion.
Discuss the core-tail decomposition, non-extensive metrics, and the KL divergence results.
Explain the Lévy Walk vs. Lévy Flight classification based on the ballistic peaks in the Lévy-Lorentz gas.
Discuss the effective fractional operator derivation for Sisyphus cooling and the boundary delineation in Fourier space.
Present the mechanism-to-operator mapping and synthesize the minimal effective theory based on the summary plots.

Explicitly address the question of which single mechanism (or combination) constitutes the minimal effective theory, citing the Lévy fidelity scores, DFA exponents, and KL divergence values from previous steps.
Discuss whether the CTRW with Lévy jumps or the pure Lévy stable process itself is the most parsimonious description.
Ensure all qualitative and quantitative results, interpretations of plots, and key statistics are included and properly referenced.
